text.title: Internal RFC Template Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Internal RFC Template application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Five sections only: Motivation, Proposal, Alternatives, Open Questions, Status. Anything longer than 5 sections drifts to design-doc.
rule.rationale: Empirical: Rust + Oxide RFC formats converged on 5 sections; longer formats reduce author + reviewer adoption.

rule.statement: Status moves through: Draft → Discussion → Accepted | Rejected | Superseded. Each transition is recorded with date + decider.
rule.rationale: Without explicit status, RFCs become zombie docs; reviewers don't know if commentary is wanted.

rule.statement: Comment window ≥7 days; can be extended; cannot be skipped. Forced-accept = process abandonment.
rule.rationale: Comment window is the protection against unilateral decisions; skipping = ADR-equivalent without ADR's finality.

rule.statement: When an RFC is superseded, both old and new link to each other. Forward-only links rot.
rule.rationale: Bidirectional linking is the only way readers find current state from old docs.

rule.statement: Rejected RFCs are archived, not deleted. Their rationale teaches the team why an approach didn't work.
rule.rationale: Deleting rejected proposals destroys institutional learning; archive preserves it.
